text: Selective modification of nitrogen heteroaromatics can lead to the discovery of new tools and increase the pace of drug discovery. While methods that increase or decrease the size of the heteroaromatic ring are being developed, the direct exchange of single core atoms is relatively rare. Here, we describe the <sup>14</sup>N → <sup>15</sup>N isotopic exchange of several aromatic nitrogen heterocycles. The <sup>14</sup>N → <sup>15</sup>N nitrogen isotope transmutation involves triflylation of the heteroaromatic substrate, followed by a ring-opening/ring-closure sequence mediated by <sup>15</sup>N-aspartate to facilitate the <sup>14</sup>N → <sup>15</sup>N exchange of the nitrogen atom of the heterocycle. The formation of the isolable <sup>15</sup>N-succinyl intermediate is crucial to this transformation as it allows the intermediate to undergo an elimination reaction to afford the <sup>15</sup>N isotopologues of the heterocycles in high yields and with excellent isotopic incorporation.
